problem_summary: 2019 MCM/ICM Problem F (policy): assess the impact of a decentralized digital currency on sovereign currencies, government behavior, and the global trade/financial system, and recommend policy. Team 1905127 built a three-level "General Digital Currency Circulation Model" (individual / national / international) grounded in monetary economics, and produced an Outstanding (O-award) paper plus a one-page policy memo to national leaders.
models_used: Fisher's equation of exchange (PY = Mv) used to derive price/volatility of digital currency via incremental form ΔP = ΔMv/Y, splitting demand into transactional vs speculative
Keynesian theory of money demand (speculative-demand decomposition; speculative demand tied to exchange-rate expectation, interest-rate expectation, and crowd attention)

Diamond-Mortensen-Pissarides (DMP) search-and-matching model, EXTENDED to a dual-currency (legal 'flat' vs digital) closed economy with Bellman-style value equations rV_o, rV_f, rV_d and acceptance probabilities λ, μ; solved for equilibrium acceptance thresholds λ̂, μ̂

Intertemporal government tax-maximization model: max z = Σ g_t/(1+r)^t, with per-period revenue g_t built from tax rates t_f, t_d, matching efficiency e(GDP), and a regulatory/monetary-sovereignty cost term C(μ)

Bacchetta & van Wincoop (2002/2005) currency-denomination-of-trade model, EXTENDED to add digital currency as a third settlement currency; uses second-order Taylor / Jensen-type expansion of expected utility of profit EU(Π) over exchange-rate variance σ² to compare settlement choices

Mundell-McKinnon Optimal Currency Area (OCA) theory, adapted into a two-track 'Supranational Monetary System Model' (digital currency issued by a supranational bank by demand share + retained sovereign currencies), contrasted against the Mundell-Fleming-Dornbusch (MFD) model

Purchasing Power Parity (PPP) / interest-rate-parity relation to set the floating exchange rate between each sovereign currency and the universal digital currency (ε expressed via relative real interest and inflation rates)

Multivariate OLS linear regression (Stata) of country digital-currency acceptance μ_i on price level P, output Y, trading volume TR, public attention IN, legal attitude LE, network security SF, price risk PR — as empirical validation of the structural model

Monte Carlo simulation of US trade split between fiat and digital currency markets (1994-2016 fit, 10-year forecast), showing convergence of digital market share to ~65.21% independent of initial share, and collapse when the supranational bank loses control
Quadratic polynomial curve fitting (MATLAB polyfit, degree 2) of US total trade volume to forecast future trade for the Monte Carlo simulation
key_techniques: Three-tier micro-to-macro decomposition (individual / national / international) that maps directly onto the three sub-questions of the prompt

Borrowing and EXTENDING canonical economics models rather than inventing ad hoc ones: DMP → dual-currency, Bacchetta-van Wincoop → third currency, OCA → two-track supranational system — each citation gives the model academic credibility
Equilibrium / fixed-point analysis on the acceptance proportions, yielding a clean qualitative taxonomy (stable coexistence vs vicious-circle marginalization vs convergence to full adoption)

Anchoring otherwise-unobservable equilibrium thresholds to real reference countries: Zimbabwe (assumed 100% foreign/digital acceptance → legal-tender threshold λ̂) and USA (assumed 100% dollar acceptance → digital threshold μ̂), then classifying ~130 countries against those thresholds

Mapping abstract model parameters to measurable proxies (δ = log GDP, pc = inflation rate, p_o-d = Bitcoin's share of money supply, risk = GCI cyber-security rank, attention = Google Trends Bitcoin search heat, volatility = 24h BTC price swing)
Cross-validation strategy: derive country outcomes structurally from the DMP thresholds, THEN re-confirm direction/significance of drivers with an independent OLS regression

Robustness via Monte Carlo: showing the ~65% equilibrium share is invariant to random initial conditions, then showing the regime collapse when supranational regulation fails — a built-in sensitivity/scenario analysis
Strong visualization: world choropleth map (both/legal-only/digital-only/missing) and convergence line charts for stable vs imbalance regimes
why_it_won: "From a judge's view it wins on intellectual ambition matched to rigor. The policy prompt is open-ended and easy to answer with hand-waving; this team instead imposed a disciplined micro-to-macro architecture and populated each layer with a real, citable economics model (Fisher, Keynes, DMP, Bacchetta-van Wincoop, Mundell OCA, PPP). Critically, it doesn't just cite these models — it DERIVES new results (dual-currency equilibrium thresholds, a third-currency settlement criterion, a two-track supranational system that repairs the MFD trilemma) and then makes them EMPIRICAL: real data for ~130 countries, a Stata OLS regression as independent validation, and a Monte Carlo showing a robust ~65% equilibrium plus a collapse scenario. The combination of theoretical depth, a falsifiable world-map prediction, an honest weaknesses section, and a clean stand-alone policy memo to national leaders is exactly the 'complete, plausible, well-communicated, self-aware' package that earns Outstanding on a policy problem."
section_highlights: "Front matter: a dense Summary Sheet that previews all three sub-models and their punchlines, plus a separate one-page POLICY RECOMMENDATION memo addressed 'To: National leaders' — directly satisfying the deliverable. Overview (1.3) includes a full mermaid-style flowchart tying every model to the three levels. Section 3 is the theory core: 3.1 individual DMP equilibrium with explicit value equations and a λ/μ case taxonomy; 3.2 the intertemporal tax-max model with the discounted-revenue objective; 3.3 the trade-profit-under-volatility derivation and the supranational OCA system. Section 4 is the empirical payoff: threshold-anchoring to Zimbabwe/USA, the parameter-to-proxy table, the OLS regression table (n=53, R²=0.282), the world-equilibrium choropleth (Fig 3), and the Monte Carlo convergence vs collapse charts (Figs 4-5). Section 4.3 extends to commercial-bank disruption, monetary-policy independence, infant-industry protection, and Eurozone-style convergence failure. Section 5 gives an explicit, candid Strengths/Weaknesses split and future work; Appendix ships the actual MATLAB polyfit and world-map drawing code."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Self-admitted: the sub-models are loosely coupled ('the framework is relatively large and the relationships of sub models are relatively loose'), so there is no single unified system — results are stitched, not jointly solved
Weak empirical foundation: a global decentralized digital market does not exist, so the structural model can't be validated against reality; macro currency-choice theory is acknowledged as abstract
The OLS validation is thin: only n=53 countries, R²=0.282, and all three risk variables (LE, SF, PR) are statistically insignificant — the authors lean on 'economic significance' to rescue them

Threshold-anchoring is a strong, unargued assumption: treating Zimbabwe as a 100% foreign/digital-acceptance equilibrium and the USA as a 100% dollar equilibrium drives the entire 130-country classification, and a wrong anchor would shift every prediction

Proxy validity is shaky: Bitcoin's 24h share, Google Trends search heat, and 24h price swings are noisy stand-ins for deep structural quantities; using Bitcoin as 'the' digital currency conflates a speculative asset with a hypothetical stable medium of exchange
The appendix US trade-volume series is a suspiciously smooth near-linear ramp (9000 → 47000), suggesting illustrative/synthetic rather than real data feeding the Monte Carlo
Circular logic risk: stability of digital currency is assumed (low volatility) to prove it becomes the trade medium, while its becoming the trade medium is what is supposed to cause the stability
English-language and notation errors throughout (typos, 'flat' for 'fiat', garbled symbols), which can cost communication points even when the math is sound
reusable_patterns: Micro → national → international tiering: for any policy/economics-flavored MCM problem, structure the model as individual-behavior → government-objective → system-level equilibrium so each tier answers one sub-question

Extend-a-canonical-model move: locate the textbook model nearest the prompt (search/matching, OCA, trade-denomination) and add ONE new dimension to it; cite the original to inherit credibility and derive a genuinely new result

Anchor unobservable thresholds to real reference cases: pick one or two real-world entities you can argue are 'at equilibrium', back out the critical parameter from them, then classify the rest of the population against it

Parameter-to-proxy table: explicitly list each abstract model parameter next to a concrete, fetchable data proxy (log GDP, inflation, market share, a public index) — makes an abstract model instantly empirical

Structural-then-statistical double validation: derive outcomes from the structural model, then run an independent regression to confirm the sign/significance of drivers; report both even if the regression is weak

Monte Carlo for robustness + a deliberate failure scenario: show the equilibrium is invariant to random initial conditions, then show the regime collapse when a key assumption (here, supranational regulation) breaks — doubles as sensitivity analysis
Ship a stand-alone policy memo as front matter for any 'advise the decision-maker' prompt, written in plain language and separable from the technical body
Include the actual solver/plotting code (MATLAB polyfit, choropleth) in an appendix to signal reproducibility
Write an honest, specific Strengths/Weaknesses section that names the real limitations (loose coupling, no real data) rather than generic caveats — judges reward self-awareness
